steps.title: Fastest path: identify the form first
steps.content: For coordinate-circle questions, read the center and radius from the equation. Identify the shifts and radius before doing algebra.

steps.title: Recall the standard form of a circle
steps.content: A circle with center $(h, k)$ and radius $r$ has equation $(x - h)^2 + (y - k)^2 = r^2$. Compare the given equation $(x - 11)^2 + (y - k)^2 = 64$ to this form.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• The center is at $(11, k)$ and the radius is 8.: From $(x - 11)^2$ the $x$-shift is 11, giving $h = 11$. From $(y - k)^2$ the $y$-shift is $k$. And $r^2 = 64$ means $r = 8$. This matches perfectly.
  • The center is at $(k, 11)$ and the radius is 8.: The coordinates of the center are reversed. The $x$-coordinate of the center is 11, not $k$.
  • The center is at $(11, k)$ and the radius is 64.: The center is correct but the radius is wrong. The equation gives $r^2 = 64$, so $r = 8$, not 64.
  • The center is at $(k, 11)$ and the radius is 64.: Both the center coordinates are reversed and the radius is wrong.
That matches the correct answer; A is correct.
